title: Spandasandoha (commentary on the Spandakārikā)
author: Kṣemarāja (title misattributes to Kallaṭa; the Spandakārikā mūla is by Vasugupta/Kallaṭa)
date_estimate: c. early 11th c. CE (Kṣemarāja fl. c. 1000-1050); the Spandakārikā it expounds is 9th c. CE
summary: The Spandasandoha is an extended exposition (sandoha) by Kṣemarāja concentrating on the opening verse(s) of the Spandakārikā. It elaborates the doctrine of spanda, the subtle creative 'vibration' or pulsation of divine consciousness (Śiva) that underlies all manifestation and cognition, and shows how recognition of this dynamic awareness yields liberation. It functions as a doctrinal and devotional gateway to the full Spanda system.
history: The mūla Spandakārikā is ascribed either to Vasugupta or to his pupil Bhaṭṭa Kallaṭa (9th c.); the record's attribution of the Spandasandoha to Kallaṭa is an error, as the Spandasandoha is the well-attested work of Kṣemarāja, disciple of Abhinavagupta. Kṣemarāja also wrote the fuller Spandanirṇaya covering the whole text; Kallaṭa's own contribution is the early Spandavṛtti.

title: Tattvacintāmaṇi (Śrītattvacintāmaṇi), chapters 1-5
author: Pūrṇānanda (Paramahaṃsa Pūrṇānanda of Bengal)
date_estimate: 16th c. CE; the work is dated 1577 CE
summary: The Śrītattvacintāmaṇi is a voluminous Śākta-tantric compendium in Sanskrit verse, organized into chapters (prakāśas), covering nearly the whole field of Tantra: initiation (dīkṣā), the subtle body and its cakras, kinds of mantra, tantric goddesses, and the worship of Tripurasundarī/Ṣoḍaśī and the Śrīcakra. Chapters 1-5 cover the opening matter including initiation, mantra-śāstra, and foundational ritual. The famous Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa is its sixth chapter.
history: Composed by Pūrṇānanda, a Bengali sādhaka, in 1577. The work runs to roughly twenty-six prakāśas; its sixth chapter (Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa) circulated independently and became internationally known through Arthur Avalon's translation 'The Serpent Power.' It is a major source for the systematized cakra-yoga of later Tantra.

title: Tattvacintāmaṇi (Śrītattvacintāmaṇi), chapters 6-10
author: Pūrṇānanda (Paramahaṃsa Pūrṇānanda of Bengal)
date_estimate: 16th c. CE; the work is dated 1577 CE
summary: This portion of Pūrṇānanda's Śākta-tantric compendium opens (chapter 6) with the celebrated Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa, the description of the six cakras of the subtle body, the kuṇḍalinī, and the ascent of Śakti to Śiva at the sahasrāra. The following chapters continue with the description of mantras, tantric female deities, and the worship of the supreme goddess Tripurasundarī. It is the most influential block of the whole work.
history: The sixth chapter (Ṣaṭcakranirūpaṇa) became a standard reference text for cakra-yoga and was translated by Arthur Avalon (John Woodroffe) as 'The Serpent Power' (1919), shaping modern Western understandings of the subtle body. The parent work was composed in 1577.

title: Tattvacintāmaṇi (Śrītattvacintāmaṇi), chapters 11-15
author: Pūrṇānanda (Paramahaṃsa Pūrṇānanda of Bengal)
date_estimate: 16th c. CE; the work is dated 1577 CE
summary: These middle chapters of Pūrṇānanda's Śākta compendium continue the systematic treatment of tantric worship: the various tantric goddesses, the deity Ṣoḍaśī, the nature and worship of the Śrīcakra (the yantra after which the work is named), and hymns (stotras) to Tripurasundarī. The material treats both the metaphysics of the goddess and detailed ritual procedure (pūjā, sādhanā).
history: Part of the c. 1577 Śrītattvacintāmaṇi of the Bengali sādhaka Pūrṇānanda, a comprehensive Sanskrit verse digest drawing together Śākta-tantric doctrine and practice; widely copied in eastern India and an important witness to Śrīvidyā in Bengal.

title: Tattvacintāmaṇi (Śrītattvacintāmaṇi), chapters 16 to end
author: Pūrṇānanda (Paramahaṃsa Pūrṇānanda of Bengal)
date_estimate: 16th c. CE; the work is dated 1577 CE
summary: The closing chapters (16 through the final, roughly the 26th prakāśa) of Pūrṇānanda's Śākta-tantric compendium. They include the sacrifice called Jñānaḍūtī, the hymn of the thousand names (sahasranāma) of the great Tripurasundarī, and the consecration of mystical diagrams (yantra) used as protective amulets, completing the treatment of advanced Śrīvidyā ritual and theology.
history: Concludes the c. 1577 Śrītattvacintāmaṇi of Pūrṇānanda; the whole work comprises about twenty-six prakāśas and stands as one of the most comprehensive late-medieval Bengali Tantric manuals.

title: Stavacintāmaṇi
author: Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa
date_estimate: late 9th-early 10th c. CE
summary: The Stavacintāmaṇi ('Wish-fulfilling Jewel of Praise') is a hymn of about 120 invocatory verses addressed to Śiva and his Śakti as the source of creation, maintenance, and dissolution. It uniquely fuses devotional (bhakti) feeling with the metaphysics of monistic Kashmir Śaivism, expressing the lived realization of nondual Śaiva yoga through praise.
history: Composed by Bhaṭṭa Nārāyaṇa (not to be confused with the dramatist of the Veṇīsaṃhāra). The hymn was canonized within nondual Śaivism by Kṣemarāja's learned commentary, which reads its devotional verses through the technical vocabulary of Pratyabhijñā yogic experience, integrating it into the Trika canon.
related_works: commentary of Kṣemarāja on the Stavacintāmaṇi

title: Suprabhedāgama
author: anonymous/traditional (revelation ascribed to Śiva)
date_estimate: c. 7th-11th c. CE (disputed; one of the late Siddhāntāgamas)
summary: The Suprabhedāgama is one of the twenty-eight canonical Siddhāntāgamas of dualist Śaiva Siddhānta. Like other āgamas it treats the four traditional 'feet' (pādas)—doctrine (jñāna/vidyā), yoga, ritual (kriyā), and conduct (caryā)—prescribing temple construction, image installation, initiation, daily and occasional worship, and the path to liberation through Śiva's grace.
history: Traditionally counted among the five āgamas proclaimed by the Vāmadeva face of Śiva. Its dating is uncertain; like most Siddhāntāgamas its received text grew over centuries and survives mainly in South Indian (especially Tamil-region) manuscript traditions where it remains authoritative for temple ritual.
related_works: the 28 Śaiva Siddhānta Āgamas (e.g. Kāmika, Kāraṇa, Ajita, Raurava)

title: Svacchandatantra
author: anonymous/traditional (revelation; dialogue of Bhairava and Devī)
tradition: Hindu, Śaiva (Mantramārga Bhairava tantra; read nondualistically in Kashmir)
date_estimate: c. 7th c. CE (text); influential nondual reading 10th-11th c.
summary: The Svacchandatantra is a large Bhairava-tantra cast as a dialogue between the goddess Devī and Bhairava, centered on the worship of Svacchanda-bhairava ('Autonomous Bhairava') and his consort Aghoreśvarī. It expounds initiation, mantra, maṇḍala, yoga, cosmology, and ritual—including liberation- and power-oriented practices—and was a major scripture of the Mantramārga.
history: Dated to around the seventh century, it was originally read in a more dualist (Siddhānta-leaning) key. The Kashmiri exegete Kṣemarāja (10th-11th c.), disciple of Abhinavagupta, wrote the only extant commentary, the Uddyota, deliberately reinterpreting the tantra along monistic Trika lines and refuting earlier dualist commentators.

title: Tantrāloka, chapters 1-14 (āhnikas)
author: Abhinavagupta
date_estimate: c. 990-1015 CE (Abhinavagupta fl. c. 975-1025)
summary: The Tantrāloka ('Light on the Tantras') is Abhinavagupta's monumental synthesis of Trika Śaivism, presented as an exposition of the Mālinīvijayottaratantra. Its first fourteen āhnikas lay the philosophical and soteriological foundations: the nature of Śiva-consciousness and its self-manifestation, the four 'means' (upāyas) to liberation (āṇava, śākta, śāmbhava, and the no-means anupāya), the categories of bondage and grace (śaktipāta), and the metaphysics of the universe as the expansion of consciousness.
history: Composed in early-11th-century Kashmir, the Tantrāloka comprises around 5,800 verses in 37 āhnikas and is the most authoritative systematic work of nondual Kashmiri Śaivism. It was illuminated by the indispensable Viveka commentary of Abhinavagupta's pupil Jayaratha (12th-13th c.).

title: Tantrāloka, chapters 15-38 (āhnikas)
author: Abhinavagupta
date_estimate: c. 990-1015 CE (Abhinavagupta fl. c. 975-1025)
summary: These later āhnikas of the Tantrāloka turn from foundational metaphysics to the detailed practice of Trika ritual: regular and occasional worship, initiation (dīkṣā) in its many forms, the construction and use of maṇḍalas, mantra and the phonematic emanation of reality, consecration, funerary and expiatory rites, sacred geography and time, and Kaula esoteric practice. The final āhnika treats the transmission of the tradition and the structure of the work itself.
history: The concluding portion of Abhinavagupta's c. 37-chapter, ~5,800-verse magnum opus, completing the integration of scripture, ritual, and nondual philosophy. Jayaratha's Viveka commentary remains essential for understanding its dense technical material.

title: Tāratantra
author: anonymous/traditional (revelation; Śiva-Devī dialogue)
tradition: Hindu, Śākta Tantra (Daśamahāvidyā; cult of Tārā)
date_estimate: uncertain; likely late medieval (c. 11th-16th c. CE)
summary: The Tāratantra is a Śākta scripture devoted to the worship of the goddess Tārā, the second of the ten Mahāvidyās, in her forms such as Ugratārā, Ekajaṭā, and Nīlasarasvatī. It sets out her mantras, meditative visualizations (dhyāna), ritual procedure, and the distinctive Mahācīnakrama ('Tibetan/Chinese rite'), reflecting the close interface between Hindu and Buddhist Tārā cults.
history: One of several tantras (alongside Nīlatantra, Tārākalpa, Rudrayāmala material, etc.) underlying the Hindu Tārā cult that flourished especially in Bengal and Assam (Kāmākhyā). The text quotes from and is bound up with the Tārārahasya tradition; precise authorship and date are not securely established, and several differing works circulate under similar titles.

title: Tārārahasya
author: Brahmānanda (Brahmānandagiri)
tradition: Hindu, Śākta Tantra (cult of Tārā / Mahāvidyā)
date_estimate: c. 16th c. CE (disputed; Brahmānandagiri of Bengal/Kāmākhyā)
summary: The Tārārahasya ('Secret of Tārā') is a Śākta compendium guiding the pūjā and sādhanā of the goddess Tārā and her aspects (Nīlasarasvatī, Ugrā, Ekajaṭā, and related Śaktis). Across its chapters it treats daily worship, initiation, mantras, dhyāna, and the Mahācīnakrama, the special code of ritual conduct peculiar to Tārā worship. It is one of the principal practical handbooks of the Tārā cult.
history: Compiled by Brahmānandagiri, an ascetic associated with Kāmākhyā who drew on numerous earlier sources (Tārāsāra, Nīlatantra, Mahācīnatantra, Rudrayāmala, etc.). Estimates put the text at roughly 2,500 ślokas. Its popularity was later rivaled by the Tārārahasyavṛttikā of Śaṅkara Āgamācārya of Bengal.

title: Tārārahasyavṛttikā
author: Śaṅkara Āgamācārya (of Bengal)
tradition: Hindu, Śākta Tantra (cult of Tārā / Mahāvidyā)
date_estimate: late medieval (after the Tārārahasya; c. 16th-17th c. CE)
summary: The Tārārahasyavṛttikā is a commentary/gloss expanding on Brahmānandagiri's Tārārahasya. Across its chapters it elaborates the daily worship and initiation of Tārā, the mantras and meditative descriptions of the goddess, and the Mahācīnakrama 'Tibetan method' of ritual conduct, providing detailed prose explanation (including the iconographic description of Tārā's image quoted by other tantras).
history: Written by Śaṅkara Āgamācārya of Bengal, this commentary came to overshadow the base text in popularity and is cited as an authority by later Tārā literature (e.g. the Tāratantra draws its prose description of Tārā's image from it). It is a key witness to the systematization of Tārā worship in eastern India.

title: Vāmakeśvarīmata (Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇava)
author: anonymous/traditional (revelation)
tradition: Hindu, Śākta Tantra (Kaula Śrīvidyā; cult of Tripurasundarī)
date_estimate: c. 9th-11th c. CE (disputed; some place it before the 9th c.)
summary: The Vāmakeśvarīmata, better known as the Nityāṣoḍaśikārṇava, is the root tantra of the Kaula Śrīvidyā tradition centered on the goddess Tripurasundarī. It expounds the Śrīcakra and its goddesses (the Nityā deities), the fifteen-syllable mantra (pañcadaśākṣarī), and the ritual and yogic worship of the supreme Śakti, drawing on Trika and Kubjikā doctrine.
history: Counted as the earliest principal Tantra of Kaula Śrīvidyā and foundational for the entire later tradition. It received important commentaries, notably the Ṛjuvimarśinī of Śivānanda and Jayaratha's Vivaraṇa. Its companion text within the lineage is the Yoginīhṛdaya.

title: Varivasyārahasya
author: Bhāskararāya Makhin
date_estimate: early-to-mid 18th c. CE (Bhāskararāya c. 1690-1785)
summary: The Varivasyārahasya ('Secret of Worship') is Bhāskararāya's treatise expounding the manifold esoteric meanings of the Śrīvidyā fifteen-syllable mantra (pañcadaśākṣarī) and its worship. Through detailed analysis of the mantra's letters, deities, and contemplative correspondences it concludes by demonstrating that Śrīvidyā ultimately teaches the Upaniṣadic identity of the individual self with the supreme Self. The author supplied his own commentary, the Prakāśa.
history: Bhāskararāya Makhin was an 18th-century polymath and Śrīvidyā authority who served as a court paṇḍita at Thanjavur and authored some forty works. He bridged Vedic orthodoxy and Tantric esotericism and advocated the right-hand (dakṣiṇa) path of Śrīvidyā. He also commented on the Lalitāsahasranāma, Saundaryalaharī, and Bhāvanopaniṣad.

title: Vātūlanāthasūtra
author: attributed to Vātūlanātha (Siddha)
date_estimate: uncertain; probably after the 12th c. CE
summary: The Vātūlanāthasūtra is a short set of about thirteen terse aphorisms belonging to the Kashmiri Krama tradition. They expound the highest nondual realization—the 'great spontaneous leap' (mahāsāhasa-vṛtti) into Śiva-consciousness—and the supreme state of unmediated identity with the absolute, framed as revelation received from the Yoginīs.
history: Authorship is ascribed to the Kaula Siddha Vātūlanātha, said to have lived after the 12th century. The sūtras survive with the Sanskrit commentary (vṛtti) of Anantaśaktipāda, who frames them as a direct revelation from enlightened Yoginīs; neither sūtras nor commentary can be securely dated. They preserve rare documentary evidence of the orally transmitted Krama teachings.
related_works: Vṛtti of Anantaśaktipāda (commentary on the Vātūlanāthasūtra)

title: Viśvasāratantra
author: anonymous/traditional (revelation)
date_estimate: uncertain; medieval (likely 2nd millennium CE)
summary: The Viśvasāratantra ('Tantra of the Essence of the Universe') is a tantric scripture treating cosmogony, the ordering of the cosmic regions, salvation, and the stages of spiritual practice. It is noted for the macrocosm-microcosm maxim 'yad ihāsti tad anyatra, yan nehāsti na tat kvacit' ('what is here is elsewhere; what is not here is nowhere'), and it gives a fuller account of the stages of sādhanā than some related tantras such as the Kulārṇava.
history: Listed among the recognized tantras (e.g. in the Mahāmokṣatantra's enumeration) and cited in Tantric bibliographies; it is also referenced in connection with Ayurvedic/medical material. Its authorship, date, and full text are not securely established and it survives mainly through manuscript references and quotations.

title: Advayatārakopaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: disputed; broadly 1st millennium CE (Flood: c. 100 BCE-300 CE for Yoga Upaniṣads; possibly later, up to ~14th c.)
summary: The Advayatārakopaniṣad ('Upaniṣad on the Nondual Deliverer/Star') is a short Yoga Upaniṣad teaching tāraka-yoga, a meditative practice of inner light and the 'crossing-over' to the nondual Brahman. It describes the yogin's perception of inner luminous signs, the śāmbhavī mudrā (gaze), and culminates in the realization of the qualified and unqualified Brahman and the role of the guru.
history: One of the twenty Yoga Upaniṣads, transmitted within the Śukla-Yajurveda corpus and appearing in the Muktikā canon of 108 Upaniṣads. Like other minor Upaniṣads it is anonymous and of uncertain date; it integrates Vedāntic nondualism with Haṭha/Laya yoga practice.

title: Amṛtabindūpaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional
tradition: Hindu, Vedānta / Yoga (Yoga Upaniṣad; Bindu group)
date_estimate: disputed; broadly 1st millennium CE (Flood: c. 100 BCE-300 CE)
summary: The Amṛtabindūpaniṣad ('Upaniṣad of the Drop of Immortality'), also called Brahmabindu Upaniṣad, is a very short Yoga Upaniṣad of about twenty-two verses on the control of the mind as the means to liberation. It teaches that the mind alone causes bondage and freedom, distinguishes the pure (soundless) from the impure mind, and expounds the practice of dissolving thought into the silent (aśabda) Brahman through OM.
history: One of the most widely transmitted minor Upaniṣads, attached to the Atharvaveda (or Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda in some lists) and included in the Muktikā canon. It shares a number of verses with the Amṛtanāda and other Bindu/Yoga Upaniṣads, and is anonymous and of uncertain date.

title: Amṛtanādopaniṣad
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: disputed; broadly 1st millennium CE (Flood: c. 100 BCE-300 CE for Yoga Upaniṣads)
summary: The Amṛtanādopaniṣad ('Upaniṣad of the Sound/Bliss of Immortality') is a short Yoga Upaniṣad describing a graded yogic path to liberation. It sets out a sixfold yoga (pratyāhāra, dhyāna, prāṇāyāma, dhāraṇā, tarka, samādhi), meditation on OM as the means of crossing beyond the perishable, and contemplation culminating in the realization of the immortal Self.
history: Attached to the Kṛṣṇa-Yajurveda and counted among the twenty Yoga Upaniṣads in the Muktikā canon of 108 Upaniṣads. Anonymous and of uncertain date, it shares material with the Amṛtabindu and other Yoga Upaniṣads and reflects the fusion of Vedāntic and yogic teaching characteristic of this corpus.
